core_behavior: The system must intercept all user-supplied input at the point of entry — before any business logic, data storage, or downstream processing occurs — and subject it to a defined set of validation rules.
Input that passes all applicable rules is forwarded to the processing pipeline.
Input that fails one or more rules is rejected, and the system returns structured error feedback to the caller without invoking any downstream processing.

Validation is non-destructive by default: the system must not silently modify, truncate, or coerce input values during validation unless an explicit normalization step is declared as part of the validation contract.
All validation errors across all fields are collected and returned in a single response (fail-all, not fail-fast), unless the system is explicitly configured for fail-fast mode.
inputs: HTTP request bodies (JSON, XML, multipart/form-data, URL-encoded)
HTTP query parameters and path parameters
HTTP headers (where declared as validated fields)
Each field may be validated against presence rules: required, optional, conditionally required
Each field may be validated against type rules: string, integer, float, boolean, array, object, date, UUID
Each field may be validated against format rules: email, URL, ISO 8601 date, E.164 phone, regex pattern
Each field may be validated against length/size rules: min length, max length, exact length, min/max byte size
Each field may be validated against range rules: numeric min/max (inclusive/exclusive), date range
Each field may be validated against enumeration rules: value must belong to a defined set
Each field may be validated against uniqueness rules: value must not already exist in the data store (async check)
Each field may be validated against referential integrity rules: value must reference an existing entity
Each field may be validated against structural rules: array min/max item count, object required keys
Each field may be validated against cross-field rules: field A required only when field B equals value X
Each field may be validated against content safety rules: no null bytes, no control characters (unless explicitly permitted)
outputs: The validated (and optionally normalized) input object is passed to the processing layer on the success path.
No validation-specific response is returned to the caller on the success path; the processing layer's normal response applies.
An internal audit log entry is written on the success path.
The system returns a structured error payload before any processing occurs on the failure path.
HTTP context failure response: HTTP 422 Unprocessable Entity with Content-Type application/json containing status, code, message, errors array, request_id, and timestamp fields.

Each error entry in the errors array contains: field (dot-notation field path), rule (rule name), value (rejected value or '[REDACTED]' for sensitive fields), and message (human-readable description).
Sensitive fields (passwords, tokens, PII) must have their value replaced with '[REDACTED]' in error responses.
CLI context: errors written to stderr in human-readable format; exit code 2.
Message queue context: message is moved to a dead-letter queue with the error payload attached as metadata.
Internal RPC context: error struct returned with equivalent field-level detail.
assumptions: Validation schema is declarative and externally defined. Each endpoint or operation has an associated schema (e.g., JSON Schema, OpenAPI, or a proprietary DSL) that specifies which rules apply to which fields.
The validation engine reads the schema at startup and caches it.
Normalization is a declared, pre-validation step. Allowed normalizations must be explicitly declared as part of the validation contract.
failure_modes: Input that fails one or more rules is rejected, and the system returns structured error feedback to the caller without invoking any downstream processing.
CLI errors written to stderr in human-readable format; exit code 2.
Message queue messages that fail validation are moved to a dead-letter queue with the error payload attached as metadata.
Internal RPC returns an error struct with equivalent field-level detail on validation failure.
edge_cases: Conditionally required fields: field A required only when field B equals value X.
Sensitive fields (passwords, tokens, PII) must have their value replaced with '[REDACTED]' in error responses.
Content safety: no null bytes, no control characters unless explicitly permitted.
Uniqueness check is an async check against the data store.
The system may be explicitly configured for fail-fast mode instead of the default fail-all mode.
security_constraints: Sensitive fields (passwords, tokens, PII) must have their value replaced with '[REDACTED]' in error responses.
Content safety validation: no null bytes, no control characters (unless explicitly permitted).
